claim: Simple derives from Latin sim-plex meaning 'one fold' or 'one braid'
exact_quote: the roots of this word are sim and plex, and that means one fold or one braid or twist

claim: Easy derives from Latin root of adjacent meaning 'to lie near' or 'to be nearby'
exact_quote: from the Latin word that is the root of adjacent, which means to lie near and to be nearby

claim: Simple is an objective notion - you can look at code and see interleaving
exact_quote: if something is interleaved or not, that's sort of an objective thing. You can probably go and look and see

claim: Easy is relative - depends on the person
exact_quote: easy is always going to be, you know, easy for whom or hard for whom. It's a relative term

claim: Simplicity is about lack of interleaving, not cardinality
exact_quote: What matters for simplicity is that there is no interleaving, not that there is only one thing

claim: Complect means to interleave, entwine, or braid
exact_quote: It means to interleave or entwine or braid

claim: Complexity undermines understanding - intertwined things must be considered together
exact_quote: fundamentally this complexity, and by complexity I mean this braiding together of things, is going to limit our ability to understand our systems

claim: Simplicity is a choice requiring constant vigilance
exact_quote: Simplicity is a choice. It's your fault if you don't have a simple system

claim: Easy is fast to write; Simple is fast to change
exact_quote: Easy is fast to write. Simple is fast to change.

claim: Easy measures friction at the point of creation, not maintenance
exact_quote: But easy is about the person doing the work. It's a measure of friction at the point of creation, not at the point of understanding, maintenance, or failure.

claim: Simple solutions are harder to write and easier to maintain; easy solutions are faster to write and harder to maintain
exact_quote: Simple solutions are often harder to write and easier to maintain. Easy solutions are often faster to write and harder to maintain.

claim: YAGNI optimizes the Easy axis; SOLID optimizes the Simple axis
exact_quote: The judgment criterion for YAGNI is 'do we need it now?' ... The SOLID principles are about not intertwining concerns—a principle that optimizes the Simple axis.

claim: Nobody gets promoted for the complexity they avoided
exact_quote: Nobody gets promoted for the complexity they avoided.

claim: Complexity looks smart - promotion systems reward visible output
exact_quote: Complexity looks smart. Not because it is, but because our systems are set up to reward it.

claim: Easy will always trump simple due to production pressure
exact_quote: no matter how many people listen to this talk and agree with it, easy will always win out over simple

claim: Complex systems are inevitable and can work well (biological analogy)
exact_quote: I think biological systems are the most complex systems that we humans have encountered. And yet, they work astonishingly well.

claim: Complexity has a ratchet — systems rarely get simpler without explicit effort
exact_quote: complexity will increase unless explicit work is done to reduce it

claim: Adding anything increases total complexity; it's a trade-off
exact_quote: When you add anything at all, you are first and foremost increasing total complexity. It is just a tradeoff.

claim: Easy measures doing; simple measures understanding
exact_quote: Easy means you can do with little effort. Simple means you can understand what you do with little effort.

claim: Promotion-driven architecture increases system complexity up to 200%
exact_quote: System complexity: Business-driven → Promotion-driven, Delta: +200%

claim: Architectural complexity correlates with more bug-fixing LOC vs feature LOC
exact_quote: the more complex the architecture is, the more LOC is spent on bug-fixing, rather than adding new features

claim: Speed-first execution produces declining velocity; architecture-aware produces increasing velocity; crossover at month 4-6
exact_quote: Speed-first execution produces a declining velocity curve — fast at first, progressively slower. Architecture-aware execution produces an increasing velocity curve — slower at first, progressively faster. The crossover point is typically around month 4-6.

claim: Software engineering has an iron cross of trade-offs: simplicity, generality, efficiency, punctuality
exact_quote: an important trade space is between simplicity, generality, efficiency, and punctuality

claim: Simple tools can push complexity to users rather than managing it
exact_quote: simple tools seem to do worse because by being locally simple they push more concerns out to the user to manage

claim: Complexity is the ceiling in the age of AI coding — simple systems compound AI leverage
exact_quote: The teams that get the most out of AI will not be the ones that generate the most code. They will be the ones whose systems stay simple enough that a machine can move quickly through them without breaking them.

claim: Connascence framework classifies coupling along strength, locality, and degree axes
exact_quote: classifies coupling along three axes: strength, locality, and degree

claim: Complexity must be earned by measurable pain and paid for with controls
exact_quote: Complexity must be earned by measurable pain, and paid for with controls.

claim: Speed and durability are calibration decisions, not ideological positions
exact_quote: Speed and durability are calibration decisions, not ideological positions.

claim: Readability is a surface property; simplicity is a structural property
exact_quote: Readability is a surface property. It describes how easily a human can parse what code says. Simplicity is a structural property.

claim: Simplicity gets executed; complexity gets talked about
exact_quote: Simplicity gets executed. Complexity gets talked about.
